coredata.dc:title: Magnolia denudata leaf-derived near-infrared carbon dots as fluorescent nanoprobes for palladium(Ⅱ) detection and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Yunting
Li, Danni
Liu, Jinrui
Du, Fangkai
Tan, Xuecai
Zhou, Yuzhu
Liu, Shaogang
Xu, Weihua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an eco-friendly unique approach was used to synthesize near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) from Magnolia denudata leaves. The as-prepared NIR-CDs showed a stable near-infrared emission with peak at about 678 nm and narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM), which was utilized as a fluorophore in a fluorescent nanoprobe after modification with Pluronic F-127 for detection of palladium ion (Pd2+). The resulting nanoprobe diaplayed admirable performances for detecting Pd2+ including high selectivity, excellent water solubility, and stability. The fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe was selectivity quenched by Pd2+ within the scope of 0–200 μM. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) was 85.3 nM. Moreover, the constructed nanoprobe had an approving assay performance in real water samples. Finally, the nanoprobe demonstrated low toxicity and could be utilized to monitor Pd2+ in Li-7 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Pd2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of nanoprobe to Pd2+
Detection of Pd2+ in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of NIR-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of NIR-CDs. (c) XPS survey spectra of NIR-CDs. The high-resolution (d) C1s XPS, (e) N1s XPS, and (f) O1s spectra of NIR-CDs, respectively.

(a) Absorption spectra of NIR-CDs in acetone solution. (b) FL emission spectra of NIR-CDs in acetone solution at different excitation wavelengths. (c) FL emission spectra of NIR-CDs in different solvents. (d) FL emission spectra of NIR-CDs in ethanol solution at different water contents. (e) FL emission spectra of NIR-CDs in DMSO solution at different excitation wavelengths. (f) FL emission spectra of NIR-CDs in DMSO solution at different water contents. (50 mg·L-1 for NIR-CDs).

(a) The response time of the nanoprobe to Pd2+ (200 μM for Pd2+). (b) Fluorescence spectra change of the nanoprobe upon exposure to different concentrations of Pd2+. (c) FL responses of the nanoprobe towards various metal ions. (d) FL responses of the nanoprobe towards various metal ions in the presence of Pd2+. (200 μM for various metal ions, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of nanoprobe at 678 nm in the absence and presence of Pd2+, respectively. n = 3).

Fluorescence microscope images of Li-7 cells in bright field, red channel, and superposition diagram, respectively: a) Blank group, b) Cell imaging of nanoprobes, c) Cell imaging of nanoprobe after adding 80 μM Pd2+, d) Cell imaging of nanoprobe after adding 160 μM Pd2+.

(a) Synthetic route of NIR-CDs and their fluorescence response under 413 nm excitation. (b) Illustration of the modification process of the nanoprobe and its fluorescence response under Pd2+ existence.

head.ce:title: Magnolia denudata leaf-derived near-infrared carbon dots as fluorescent nanoprobes for palladium(Ⅱ) detection and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Engineering of Forest Products, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Engineering of Forest Products, Key Laboratory of Guangxi Colleges and Universities for Food Safety and Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University for Nationalities, Nanning 530006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Engineering of Forest Products, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Engineering of Forest Products
Key Laboratory of Guangxi Colleges and Universities for Food Safety and Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Engineering of Forest Products, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Engineering of Forest Products, Key Laboratory of Guangxi Colleges and Universities for Food Safety and Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University for Nationalities, Nanning 530006, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an eco-friendly unique approach was used to synthesize near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) from Magnolia denudata leaves. The as-prepared NIR-CDs showed a stable near-infrared emission with peak at about 678 nm and narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM), which was utilized as a fluorophore in a fluorescent nanoprobe after modification with Pluronic F-127 for detection of palladium ion (Pd2+). The resulting nanoprobe diaplayed admirable performances for detecting Pd2+ including high selectivity, excellent water solubility, and stability. The fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe was selectivity quenched by Pd2+ within the scope of 0–200 μM. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) was 85.3 nM. Moreover, the constructed nanoprobe had an approving assay performance in real water samples. Finally, the nanoprobe demonstrated low toxicity and could be utilized to monitor Pd2+ in Li-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of chemical and petroleum industry, palladium (Pd), has become an important metallic catalyst in recent years [1]. Because of its effective catalytic performance, it has been broadly used in many fields, including drug discoveries, jewellry, automobiles, and fuel cells, etc. [2]. However, the wide use of palladium in various materials and pharmacies has also raised the residual the palladium emissions in our environments including water resources, soil, and plants. On the other hand, the remained palladium is exceedingly detrimental to human body since it can combine with biological substances such as thiol-containing amino acids, DNA, proteins, and hampers cellular processes; thus aberrant accumulation of palladium is closely related to the clinical etiology of cancer [3,4]. Furthermore, Pd2+ is the largest oxidation state of the palladium which may be existence in the environment or living cells. Consequently, it is desirable to detect and image Pd2+ in the living and environmental systems.

To date, various methods have been designed to detect Pd2+, for instance atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), X-ray fluorescence, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), etc. [5–7]. However, these techniques universally suffer from typical shortcomings including time-consuming sample preparation, expensive instruments, and proficient operation, which critically restrict their further use [8,9]. In contrast, fluorescent techniques are most popular because of the high selectivity, real-time, and in situ imaging [10–13]. Up to now, many different types of fluorescent probes have been designed for detecting the level of Pd2+ [14–27]. In particular, there have been many recent reports of the use of organic dyes [14–23], conjugated polymers [24,25], metal–organic frameworks [26,27]. However, their complicated design and synthesis coupled with possible cytotoxicity and environmentally-unfriendly factor may be a negative role in the practical application. Thus, desirable probes for Pd2+ detection should bear features as biocompatibility, non-cytotoxicity, low-cost, and ease of operation.

Carbon dots (CDs) have become a hotspot in the research area of fluorescent material due to their distinctive and fascinating optical property, high biocompatibility, facile preparation and modification [28,29]. However, emissions originated from the majority of the published CDs located at blue or green wavelengths, which may be hindrances in their applications [30,31]. The fluorescent emitting at near-infrared (NIR) region benefits from reducing autofluorescence and increasing tissue-penetration depths, acquiring high imaging contrast and spatial resolution [32]. To date, only a few reports on the synthesis of near-infrared emission CDs (NIR-CDs) was published [33–35]. For example, Lan et al. produced S, Se co-doped CDs with NIR emissive windows excited by one- and two-photon modes [36]. Yang’s group proposed two kinds of near-infrared emissive CDs using o-phenylenediamine as precursors [37,38]. Qu and co-workers reported near-infrared CDs with largest emissive wavelength at 760 nm via modification treatment of functional molecules or polymers [39]. Despite the impressive progress obtained in these previous works, there is still an enormous need for exploiting novel methods to prepare NIR-CDs with narrow FWHM. In addition, their synthesis strategies ordinarily need expensive or even toxic substances like the compounds containing aromatic ring as the carbon sources, which is detrimental the inexpensively and environment-friendly formation of NIR-CDs [40]. Very recently, Yang’s group also developed a simple prepared method for CDs with a deep-red emission and the narrowest FWHM using Taxus leaves as a carbon source [41]. However, exploration of NIR-CDs produced by nature resources as fluorescent probes to monitor and image analytes in living cells remains at the early stage.

Magnolia denudata leaves, as a sort of easily accessible, extensively distributed, and renewable biomass, are rich in plenty of hydrocarbon compounds required for carbon dots preparation. Herein, we demonstrate a novel fluorescent nanoprobe using NIR-CDs for detecting and imaging Pd2+ in water and living cells. As displayed in Scheme 1 , the NIR-CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis of magnolia denudata leaves with excellent photostability along with excitation-independent NIR emission and a narrow FWHM of 25 nm. The hydrophobic NIR-CDs were encapsulated in micelles formed through self-assembly of non-ionic surfactant Pluronic F-127 and emitted strong red fluorescence. The presence of Pd2+ caused the binding interaction of NIR-CDs with Pd2+, thereby leading to the quenching of the NIR-CDs’ fluorescence and attaining quantitative analysis of Pd2+ in aqueous media. The nanoprobe revealed outstanding selectivity, and good water solubility. What’s more, the nanoprobe could also be utilized to image Pd2+ in living cells.

Magnolia denudata leaves were collected from the campus of Guangxi University for Nationalities. Chloride salts of metal ion (Li+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cr3+, In3+), and nitrate salts of metal ion (Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Li-7 cells were purchased from CellCook Biotech. Ultrapure (Milli-Q) water was used in all the measurements and common reagents are analytical grade.

Morphology images of the NIR-CDs were captured by transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai G2 F20). A spectrophotometer (Magna-IR550) was operated to obtain the FT-IR spectra of NIR-CDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectra were completed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer. The decomposition temperature thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was obtained on a TA Instruments TGA55 thermogravimetric analyzer. TA Instruments DSC2-01180 analyzer helps to analyse the glass transition temperature. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded with Bruker D8 Advance. The optical spectra data including fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected by a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer and Beifen-ruili UV-2100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. A Bruker AV-400 spectrometer was used to obtain nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra. Size distribution was acquired from a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS. Cytotoxicity studies were determined by a LabServ K3 TOUCH Microplate Reader. The confocal images were collected on Leica SP8. Fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield were determined by an Edinburgh Steady/transient Fluorescence Spectrometer FLS 1000-stm.

Firstly, fresh magnolia denudata leaves were washed and dried at 50 °C for 12 h. Next, dry leaves were smashed into powder by a micro mill, and then 4.0 g powder was introduced in 80 mL of acetone solution, stirred for 3 min at 25 °C. Subsequently, the suspension was added to a stainless steel autoclave with teflon-lining and reacted at 120 °C for 5 h. When cooling to the ambient temperature, the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 8000 r·min−1. The supernatant was filtered by polyethersulfone membrane with 0.22 μm pore size and a dark-green solution was obtained. The residues were purified by chromatography on silica gel with petroleum ether and ethyl acetate as the eluents. Finally, the dark green solution was collected and evaporated to obtain the dark green colloidal product NIR-CDs with a yield of 2%.

The nanoprobe was prepared using the thin-film hydration method. NIR-CDs (12.5 mg) and Pluronic F-127 (126 mg) were mixed in 250 mL of ethanol solution and sonicated for 10 min. After that, the ethanol was removed by rotating under reduced pressure to form a green mixed film. Thereafter, the above film was hydrated in deionized water, sonicated for 30 min, and freeze-dried to produce the nanoprobe.

Typically, the test systems were prepared by adding different amount of Pd2+ stock solution (50 μL) to a certain concentration of the nanoprobe dispersion before the mixture solutions were diluted with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50 mM, pH 7.4) to contain a final concentration of the nanoprobe (0.5 mg·mL−1). Next, the mixture solutions were stired for 0.5 h, and then the spectroscopic data were scanned with an excitation of 413 nm.
According to ISO 10993–5, Li-7 cells were treated by the MTT method to evaluate the cytotoxicity of nanoprobe.

Li-7 cells were seeded in a special laser confocal culture dish and cultured for 24 h before imaging. In the imaging experiment of exogenous Pd2+, cells were first co-cultured with the nanoprobe (0.55 mg·mL−1) for 30 min at 37 °C and then incubated with Pd2+ (80 and 160 μM) for 0.5 h at 37 °C. Ultimately, the cells were placed on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) to acquire images (λex = 405 nm; λem = 630–720 nm).

Transmission electron microscopy was firstly performed to testify the morphology of the prepared NIR-CDs, From TEM images, the NIR-CDs exhibited quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of 2.9 nm and had excellent dispersion and no obvious aggregation (Fig. S1a, b and d). Fig. S1c showed the NIR-CDs have a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [42]. This indicates that the prepared NIR-CDs have a certain degree of crystallinity. As shown in Fig. 1 a, NIR-CDs had a broad XRD peak between 10° and 32°, which can be ascribed to extremely unordered carbon atoms. It shows that the prepared NIR-CDs form a disordered carbon structure inside, and there is a large amount of amorphous carbon. Two sharp diffraction peaks appear in the figure, which may be that the crystal structure of cellulose in leaves has not been completely destroyed [43].

Subsequently, the elemental component and surface groups of the NIR-CDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, NIR-CDs had the obvious peaks around 3417 cm−1 and 3471 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. Bimodal at 2850 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching modes of C–H. Peaks at 1736 cm−1 were ascribed to O = C-OH stretching vibrations. The absorption peaks at 1646 cm−1 were oriented to the stretching vibrations of C = O/C = C [44]. The stretching vibration of C-N was revealed in bond at 1458 cm−1. Bimodal at 1064 cm−1 and 987 cm−1 were ascribed to the C-O-C and N–H bending vibrations. The above results represented that the prepared NIR-CDs are rich in carbonyl, hydroxy, amide, ether bond, and aromatic ring groups. XPS characterization results of the NIR-CDs are presented in Fig. 1c-f. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c) displayed three typical peaks at 285.0 eV, 532.5 eV, and 400.1 eV, respectively, which suggested that NIR-CDs were mainly composed of three elements: C (86.67 %), O (12.56 %), and N (0.78 %). The C1s spectrum in Fig. 1d revealed four characteristic peaks, which were assigned to four different types of C: C–C/C = C (284.7 eV), C-N (285.2 eV), C-O-C/C-OH (285.9 eV), and C = O/C = N (286.9 eV). The O1s band (Fig. 1e) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.3 and 532.9 eV, which are attributed to C-O-C/C-OH and O = C-OH, respectively. The binding energy of 398.5, 400.1, and 400.9 eV in the N1s band (Fig. 1f) could be attributed to pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N [45]. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2) exhibited that the remarkable chemical shifts peaks of aromatic H, pyrrole H, and pyridine [46]. This is accordance with the characterization results of XRD and FT-IR.

To further clarify the chemical structure of NIR-CDs, the TGA and DTG measurements were carried out (Fig. S3). The TGA curve (blue line in Fig. S3) presented a representative one-step weight losses process for NIR-CDs, which were stable at 150 °C, the loss of weight occurred afterward, and the final residual weight was 6.73 % up to 475 °C. This result indicated polymer chains were existence in NIR-CDs [47]. At the same time, the DTG curve suggested three-stage degradation of NIR-CDs at 248 °C, 376 °C, and 408 °C, which was ascribed to the decomposition of oxygen-containing groups, the polymer chains scission and inner cross-linked polymer chains, respectively (red line in Fig. S3) [41]. This confirmed that the NIR-CDs consisted of polymer chains.

To further verify the polymer features of NIR-CDs, the phase transitions of NIR-CDs were investigated. Two circles of the DSC thermal diagrams were obtained to remove the effect of thermal history (Fig. S4). The glass transition temperature (Tg) of NIR-CDs was −24 °C, which resulted in dried NIR-CDs in a viscous colloidal state at room temperature. As the temperature increases, NIR-CDs will gradually become a liquid state, which in agreement with the actual state. Thus, the results discussed above indicate that NIR-CDs possess individual polymer characteristics.

The UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the NIR-CDs solution were recorded to describe their spectral properties. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the UV–Vis absorption of the NIR-CDs solution revealed broad absorption under a wavelength range of 350–470 nm, with a maximum absorption peak around 413 nm. Fig. 2b displayed the PL emission of the NIR-CDs in acetone solution at different excitation wavelengths, showing a stable deep red emission with the peak at about 678 nm and narrow FWHM 25 nm. When the polarity of the solvent increased (Fig. 2c), the NIR-CDs exhibited a faint shoulder emission peak at 720 nm with the excitation of 413 nm. However, the as-prepared NIR-CDs had poor solubility in water. The PL emission spectra of NIR-CDs in water/ethanol mixed solvents with the different volume ratios of water were obtained. Under a 413 nm excitation, the solution of NIR-CDs in total ethanol were transparent liquids and displayed glaringly NIR fluorescence, whereas the turbid liquid was stepwisely weakened with increasing ratios of water on account of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect (Fig. 2d). More intuitively, the photographs in Fig. S5 showed the NIR fluorescence of the NIR-CDs dispersion also apparently decreased with an addition of water. The NIR-CDs in DMSO solution exhibited a similar fluorescence phenomenon in comparison to the acetone or ethanol solution (Fig. 2e and f), which provided the possibility for biological applications. The absolute quantum yields of NIR-CDs in acetone and DMSO were 19.80 % and 22.66 %, respectively, as measured by fluorescence steady-state transient spectrometer (Fig. S6a and b).

In order to enhance the fluorescence intensity, NIR-CDs were functionally modified. We selected amphiphilic surfactant F-127 with high stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility to convert insoluble near-infrared CDs into water-soluble micellar nanoprobes by self-assembly method [48]. As depicted in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs sharply increased in micellar solutions compared with that in the neat water solutions. Afterward, the optimal F-127 concentration was studied. As displayed in Fig. S8, with increasing of F-127, the water-solubility of NIR-CDs was enhanced, and the fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs reached the maximum in water when it reached 40 μM, and then began to decrease, possibly because the critical micelle concentration was exceeded and the package form changed [49]. As a consequence, the F-127 concentration was selected to be 40 μM in the construction of the nanoprobe. We also tested the size of the micellar probe by using DLS. The average particle size of the nanoprobe in water was around 34 nm (Fig. S9a).

Then, the stability of nanoprobes under different conditions was tested. As displayed in Fig. S9b, the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe hardly changed from pH 1.0 to 9.0, indicating that the nanoprobe had wide pH stability and could be used in the in vivo environment. In addition, even under high ionic strength (Fig. S9c) and long-time ultraviolet radiation (Fig. S9d), the fluorescence intensity ratio F/ F0 (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe at 678 nm in the absence and presence of external conditions, respectively) still had no significant change. The fluorescence steady-state transient spectrometer (Fig. S10) calculated that the absolute quantum yield of the nanoprobe in water was 7.97 %. The above results showed that nanoprobes had potential applications in analytical detection and biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of nanoprobe to Pd2+
ce:section.ce:para: The time-dependency of fluorescence quenching caused by Pd2+ was primarily tested and depicted in Fig. 3 a. With Pd2+ adding, the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 began to decrease, and after 30 min, the fluorescence intensity ratio remained unchanged. As seen in Fig. 3b and Fig. S11a, it is observed that the fluorescence of the nanoprobe gradually decreased accompanied by the dosage range increasing of Pd2+ (0–200 μM). Furthermore, based on Stern-Vollmer equation [50], there was a good linear relationship between F0/F-1 (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe at 678 nm in the absence and presence of external conditions, respectively) and Pd2+ concentration in the range of 4–18 μM and 20–200 μM. The linear regression equations were y = 0.04573x-0.13988 (R2 = 0.9855) and y = 0.02195x + 0.47389 (R2 = 0.9948), respectively (Fig. S11b and S11c). The detection limit for Pd2+ was calculated as 85.3 nM based on the formula [51].

The selectivity of the nanoprobe towards Pd2+ was investigated by recording its emission intensity ratio (F/F0) while adding a wide variety of potentially interfering metal ions. It is clear that, as shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity ratio of nanoprobe was drastically decreased, which was in prodigious contrast to a negligible decrease in fluorescence with the addition of other metal ions. In addition, the detected changes of solution color were recorded by the visual observation under UV-light (λ = 365 nm) irradiation in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. S12). These results indicated that the nanoprobe had satisfied selectivity for Pd2+, which may be ascribed to the formation of a quite stable complex by the binding of nanoprobe with Pd2+. Similar results were observed from the anti-interference experiments in the coexistence of Pd2+ and other ions, which clarify that the nanoprobe has outstanding anit-interference for Pd2+ determination (Fig. 3d). Compared with other sensors (Table S1), the nanoprobe not only has excellent specificity, but also has unique advantages, such as larger Stokes shift and narrow FWHM.

To study the PL quenching mechanism of the nanoprobe after the addition of Pd2+, several experi-ments were conducted. The fluorescence decay time of the nanoprobes with and without Pd2+ was 4.21 and 4.01 ns, respectively (Table S2). The fluorescence lifetime remained unchanged, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching possible was attributed to specific interaction between nanoprobe and Pd2+. Furthermore, quenching rate constant (Kq) were obtained to be 1.09 × 1013 L·mol−1·s−1 (4–18 μM) and 5.21 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1 (20–200 μM) based on the decay time results and stern-Volmer equation, which were far greater than the limit diffusion constant 2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1 of dynamic quenching [52]. The high Kq value indicated that the quenching mechanism is owing to a static quenching mechanism caused by the formation of a quite stable complex.

To better elucidate the quenching mechanism of Pd2+ in the nanoprobe solution, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples before and after the addition of Pd2+ were further studied. As shown in Fig. S13, after adding Pd2+, the UV–Vis absorption peak at 350 nm underwent a tremendous increase, which might result from the formation of a fairly stable complex between the nanoprobe with Pd2+ [53]. Collectively, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Pd2+ in nanoprobe solution is static quenching.

To explore the practicality of the nanoprobe for detecting Pd2+, we applied the nanoprobe to detect Pd2+ in Master Kong mineral water, tap water from the Siyuan Lake campus of Guangxi University for Nationalities, and water from Siyuan Lake, Guangxi University for Nationalities. As presented in Table S3, the recoveries of Pd2+ using the nanoprobe were 95.54 %–107.14 %, along with the relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 4.6 %. Compared to the detection result obtained by ICP-MS (Table S3), the proposed nanoprobe here has an acceptable performance. The above results indicated that the nanoprobe can be used for environmental water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pd2+ in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: In the next step, we evaluated the capability of nanoprobe to monitor Pd2+ in living cells. The cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe was evaluated using Li-7 cells by MTT assays. As shown in Fig. S14, the nanoprobe exhibited little cytotoxicity up to 800 mg·L-1 in cells after 24 h, indicating that the nanoprobe was appropriate for detecting Pd2+ in living cells. Encouraged by the aforementioned promising features, fluorescent bioimaging of intracellular Pd2+ was conducted. Fig. 4 showed the fluorescence confocal images for Li-7 cells treated with the nanoprobe before and after adding Pd2+. Li-7 cells loaded with nanoprobe for 30 min showed bright red fluorescence from the intracellular region (Fig. 4b). As expected, the live cells were co-cultured with 80 μM or 160 μM of Pd2+ for 0.5 h, and a decreased fluorescent signal was observed within the living cell (Fig. 4c and d). These results indicate that the nanoprobe is cell-permeable and used for monitoring Pd2+.

In summary, we designed a feasible and simple nanoprobe for Pd2+ analysis. NIR-CDs were prepared by solvothermal method and functionalized to make them more widely used. Bright red luminescence (luminescence center is about 678 nm) was observed, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was explored. The nanoprobe has high selectivity, narrow FWHM, larger Stokes shift, excellent stability, and low cytotoxicity. More importantly, it has been proved that it can be used for cell imaging, and analysis of actual water samples, which has broad application prospects.

Yunting Qu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Danni Li: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Jinrui Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Fangkai Du: Conceptualization, Software, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xuecai Tan: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yuzhu Zhou: Investigation, Data curation. Shaogang Liu: Methodology, Supervision. Weihua Xu: Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11904059); Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (Nos. 2018GXNSFAA281136, 2018GXNSFAA294044); Key Research and Development Project of Guangxi (No. AB18126048); The Open Fund for Key Lab of Guangdong High Property and Functional Macromolecular Materials (No. 20190019), High-level-innovation Team (Guijiaoren[2020]6), Outstanding Scholar Project of Guangxi Higher Education Institutes and the Training Program for Thousands of Backbone Young Teachers in Guangxi Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107375..

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:.
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescence turn-on sensor for the detection of palladium ions that operates through in situ generation of palladium nanoparticles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new approach for simultaneous determination of Co(Ⅱ), Ni(Ⅱ), Cu(Ⅱ) and Pd(Ⅱ) using 2-thiophenaldehyde-3-thiosemicarbazone as reagent by solid phase microextraction–high performance liquid chromatography

Malik, A new approach for simultaneous determination of Co(Ⅱ), Ni(Ⅱ), Cu(Ⅱ) and Pd(Ⅱ) using 2-thiophenaldehyde-3-thiosemicarbazone as reagent by solid phase microextraction–high performance liquid chromatography, Anal. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of platinum-group metals and lead in vegetable environmental bio-monitors by voltammetric and spectroscopic techniques: critical comparison
